title: Needle-felted mushroom gnome
subtitle: A 12 cm standing figure with a red spotted mushroom-cap hat and a grey bearded body
excerpt: Needle-felt a rounded gnome body in grey or brown merino with a pointed red mushroom-cap hat decorated with white spots. The figure stands approximately 12 cm tall on a flat wool base. An intermediate three-dimensional sculpture using only roving and a foam mat.
sourceNotes: Needle-felted gnome figures with mushroom hats are a standard intermediate sculpture project in European felting workshops. The mushroom-cap form draws on traditional Scandinavian folk-art depictions. Observation-led; no copyright-bearing source.

glossaryTerms.definition: A dry felting method that uses barbed needles to tangle and compact wool fibres into a dense form without water or heat.
Combed and drawn wool fibres in a continuous rope, ready for felting or spinning.

content.text: This gnome figure is built in two main pieces: a rounded body with a beard, and a domed mushroom-cap hat. Both pieces are made by
directly on a foam mat, then the hat is placed on the body and the join needled firm. The figure stands on a flat felt disc base that keeps it upright without a wire armature.

attrs.title: Needle safety
attrs.body: Stab straight in and out at 90 degrees into the foam only. Never lever sideways. A folded kitchen sponge protects the supporting hand.

items.name: Grey or brown merino roving for the body, 25 g
White or cream roving for the beard, 5 g
Red merino roving for the hat, 15 g
White roving for hat spots, 2 g
Felting foam mat, at least 20 x 20 cm

content.text: Take 20 g of grey roving and fold it repeatedly to form a thick oval approximately 8 x 5 cm. Needle across the whole surface for 10 minutes, rotating the piece frequently, until it compacts to approximately 6 x 4 cm and holds its shape. The base of the body should be kept flatter by needling at a shallow angle along the underside: this gives the gnome a stable standing surface. Pinch the top into a slightly rounded dome.

Take 5 g of grey roving and needle it flat on the foam mat into a disc approximately 4 cm in diameter. Work from the centre outward, keeping the disc round. Needle it until it is firm enough to hold its shape. Place the body on the disc and needle through the bottom of the body into the disc at the perimeter to bond them together. The disc base prevents the gnome from rocking.

Take a wisp of white roving approximately 10 cm long and 2 cm wide. Lay it against the front of the body below the midpoint and needle the top edge in firmly, leaving the lower portion loose. The beard should extend 3 to 4 cm below the body base; trim to shape with scissors once attached. Add a second smaller wisp above the first to build up the beard thickness at the top. Needle along the sides to keep the beard close to the body shape.

Take 15 g of red roving and fold it to form a cone approximately 8 cm tall and 5 cm across at the base. Needle the outside surface until the cone holds its shape and the tip is firm. Curve the tip slightly to the side for a traditional gnome hat silhouette. Needle the interior of the base opening to form a clean rim approximately 4 cm in diameter.

Pull tiny pinches of white roving and roll each one between wet fingertips into a small ball. Place each spot on the hat surface and needle down with 10 to 15 stabs using the fine 40-gauge needle. Space five to seven spots evenly across the dome. Each spot should be roughly 6 to 8 mm in diameter when compacted.
Step 6: Attach the hat to the body

Place the hat on top of the body and needle firmly through the rim of the hat into the body surface all the way around the join. Work in short stabbing sequences around the full circumference. The hat should sit flush on the top of the body and not wobble. Add a thin layer of body-colour roving around the join to blend the hat and body junction.

items.problem: The hat sits lopsided and tips to one side
items.cause: The body dome is uneven and the hat has settled into the lower side
items.fix: Even out the top of the body by adding wisps of roving to the lower side and needling them in until the top surface is level. Re-seat the hat before needling the join.

items.problem: The spots bleed into the hat surface and lose definition
items.cause: The spot wisps were too loose and the needling spread the fibres outward
items.fix: Use very tiny pinches and roll them into compact balls before placing. Needle from directly above with the fine needle, keeping the stabs within the spot boundary.

items.problem: The figure tips over and will not stand
items.cause: The base disc is too small or not firmly bonded to the body
items.fix: Add a larger disc of grey roving, needle it flat, and needle-bond the body to it across the full base circumference.
